participant.name: Sample Explorer
participant.context: Choosing what kind of work and life to prototype after graduation.

plans.titleSixWords: Build public work from private curiosity
plans.summary: Continue building a portfolio of writing, tools, and advisory work while taking a high-learning generalist role.

timeline.milestone: Clarify the current role search and keep publishing small tools.
Join a strong team, write weekly, and ship two public projects.
Own a visible strategy or product surface with measurable outcomes.
Turn the strongest public work into a focused advisory lane.
Lead a small team or independent practice around AI-native operations.
Have a durable body of work and enough demand to choose between employment and independence.

questionsRaised: Which role gives the best learning surface without consuming all creative energy?
What public work compounds instead of becoming scattered?

people: Talk to two operators who write publicly while holding demanding roles.
Interview one independent consultant who started from a public body of work.
experiments: Run a four-week cadence of one useful artifact per week.
Offer one lightweight advisory sprint to test demand.
reflections: Track which work creates energy after the deadline passes.
Notice whether public accountability feels clarifying or performative.

plans.titleSixWords: Learn deeply inside one ambitious company
plans.summary: Commit to a single company and use the next five years to become unusually valuable inside one domain.

timeline.milestone: Pick two domains worth going deep on and build proof of taste.
Join a mission-driven company in product, strategy, or operations.
Become the person trusted with ambiguous cross-functional problems.
Own a roadmap or operating rhythm that changes company performance.
Manage a small team and mentor other generalists.
Hold a senior operating role with deep domain credibility.

questionsRaised: Which domain would still feel interesting after the novelty fades?
Would focus feel freeing or too narrow?

people: Talk to a chief of staff who became a domain leader.
Talk to a product leader at a company with strong internal mobility.
experiments: Write a one-page strategy memo for two target domains.
Spend one week reading customer calls or primary research in each domain.
reflections: Compare the kind of problems that felt worth repeating.
Notice whether the company context matters more than the role title.

plans.titleSixWords: Make learning feel like public infrastructure
plans.summary: Build a studio around tools, essays, and guided exercises that help ambitious people think better about their lives.

timeline.milestone: Prototype one guided exercise and share it with a small group.
Launch a small library of personal strategy tools and publish the thinking behind them.
Run cohorts or workshops around the most useful frameworks.
Partner with schools, founders, or communities to adapt the tools.
Build a small team that turns reading into practical decision systems.
Operate a trusted learning studio with products, writing, and live experiences.

questionsRaised: Can this become a real business without diluting the craft?
Which audience has the strongest pull for this work?

people: Talk to a cohort-based course creator.
Talk to someone who runs a tools-for-thinking product.
experiments: Run a beta Odyssey Planning session with five people.
Publish one artifact and measure whether people actually complete it.
reflections: Watch for the difference between applause and behavior change.
Ask whether the work feels more alive when it is intimate or scalable.

commonThreads: Public work and private reflection both matter.
The strongest futures combine writing, tools, and helping people make decisions.
tensions: Depth inside one institution versus breadth across many experiments.
Stable learning surface versus entrepreneurial freedom.
nextExperiments: Run one guided Odyssey Planning session with a real user.
Interview three people living different versions of these futures.
reflectionPrompts: Which plan creates the most energy before it has status?
What would I regret not testing in the next thirty days?
